title: Prompt-Driven Development Is Creating Fragile Spring Boot Apps
author: Reyanshicodes
text:
Member-only story
Prompt-Driven Development Is Creating Fragile Spring Boot Apps
Spring Boot has always promised speed. Sensible defaults, opinionated configuration, and a huge ecosystem made it possible to ship production services quickly without drowning in boilerplate.
Recently, though, a new kind of speed has entered the workflow: prompt-driven development.
Developers ask an LLM to “create a Spring Boot REST API,” paste the result into a project, tweak it until it runs, and move on. The code compiles, endpoints respond, and tests might even pass.
And yet, many of these applications feel brittle almost immediately.
This isn’t a problem with Spring Boot itself. It’s a problem with how prompt-generated code interacts with a framework that assumes you understand what it’s doing for you.
The Illusion of Working Software
Prompt-generated Spring Boot code usually looks correct at first glance:
- Controllers annotated properly
- Services wired with
@Autowired
- Repositories extending
JpaRepository
- Configuration scattered across
application.yml
The problem isn’t syntax. It’s structure.
